claim: Local machines provide crucial capabilities to AI agents that cloud-only setups cannot match; Silicon Valley undervalues the local computer, and agents need access to the same tools as users to avoid being handicapped
actionable: When designing agent systems, prioritize giving agents access to local tools and resources rather than forcing cloud-only execution; evaluate what local capabilities your agent needs and ensure they're available
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Virtual machines provide both a safety boundary and a capability unlock for agentic systems; they allow agents to install tools (Python, Node.js) and run arbitrary scripts without requiring individual approval for each action
actionable: When deploying agents that need flexibility to solve diverse tasks, use a VM sandbox rather than restricting to pre-approved binaries; this reduces approval fatigue while maintaining containment
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Execution has become cheap enough that teams can build multiple candidate solutions quickly and test them with users rather than lengthy spec-driven development cycles
actionable: Shift from planning-heavy product development to rapid prototyping; build 3-5 competing solutions and A/B test with real users rather than debating specifications upfront
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Claude Cowork was built in 10 days by reusing existing internal components and prototypes; it's not a from-scratch build but a recombination of pre-existing pieces from the broader Anthropic platform
actionable: Identify modular, reusable components in your product ecosystem and practice rapid product assembly by combining them; this reduces iteration time dramatically
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: MCPs (Model Context Protocol) are complex to integrate; giving agents direct access to plain-language API descriptions (via markdown) is often more effective than implementing formal MCP connectors
actionable: For complex integrations, provide agents with markdown API documentation and endpoint descriptions rather than building formal MCP wrappers; let the model figure out the implementation
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Skills (as implemented in Claude Cowork) are simpler to create than MCPs because they're file-based markdown descriptions; this makes them more accessible to non-technical users and more portable across agent systems
actionable: When designing extensibility mechanisms, prefer file-based markdown skill descriptions over formal protocol connectors; this lowers the barrier to skill creation and improves portability
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: There's a tradeoff between model intelligence and scaffolding; as models improve, hyper-optimized prompt engineering and tools for specific tasks may become obsolete and represent wasted engineering effort
actionable: Before investing heavily in task-specific scaffolding (custom prompts, fine-tuned planners), assess how far away better general models are; consider giving models broader capabilities instead
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Approval exhaustion is a real UX problem; asking users to approve every single CLI command or action makes agents unusable, but fully delegating without approvals is dangerous; sandboxing is a middle ground
actionable: For agents with significant autonomy, implement sandboxing (VM isolation, network controls) to reduce approval burden; this lets users delegate without approving each step
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Anthropomorphizing the agent (treating it like a person/coworker) helps guide design decisions; asking 'what would you do if you gave a person a computer instead of email?' often reveals the right system design
actionable: When designing agent interfaces and capabilities, frame decisions through the lens of 'how would you treat a human colleague?' rather than 'what's technically simpler?'
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Platform substrate value increases as execution costs drop; as code becomes cheaper to write, having a solid platform to build on top of (with existing tools, models, agents) becomes more valuable, not less
actionable: Invest in platform foundations and extensibility mechanisms; don't assume commoditized execution means less value in platform infrastructure
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Vision capabilities unlock new patterns for agent interaction; taking screenshots and having the model interpret visual state is more effective than pure text APIs for many UI automation tasks
actionable: When building agents that interact with complex UIs, enable vision-based state observation via screenshots; this reduces the need for detailed formal API integration
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Built-in browser access (either via Chrome integration or internal preview) is a critical capability for knowledge work agents; it lets the agent see exactly what it's working on and debug visually
actionable: If building agents for knowledge work, prioritize visual feedback mechanisms (built-in browser, screenshot ability) over pure text-based tool interfaces
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Skills can be made portable by keeping them as simple file-based text/markdown; symlink or file sync mechanisms (like shared cloud folders) enable reuse across multiple agent harnesses without custom infrastructure
actionable: Design skills as plain-text markdown files; store them in shared cloud storage (Dropbox, Google Drive) and sync to multiple agent contexts to enable cross-agent portability without custom registries
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Evaluation methodology matters: evals should measure full transcript outputs (tokens + file outputs + tool calls), not just vibe-checking; systematic evals guide both training and scaffolding decisions
actionable: Design evals that capture complete agent transcripts including tool use and file outputs; use this holistic measurement to drive both model training and product scaffolding decisions
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Specialized task verticals (finance, legal, etc.) need different system prompts, tool sets, and evaluation criteria than generic agents; different use cases demand different scaffolding even if using the same underlying model
actionable: For domain-specific agents, create separate system prompts, evaluation suites, and tool collections; don't try to optimize a single prompt for multiple verticals
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: Planning tools help longer-horizon tasks; encouraging agents to create step-by-step plans (especially for knowledge work) and letting users review/edit plans before execution reduces wrong answers
actionable: For tasks longer than a few minutes, use system prompt steering to encourage planning; expose the plan to the user for approval before execution
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work

claim: General-purpose platforms beat hyper-specialized products long-term; companies that over-optimize for narrow use cases (e.g., Node.js-only or React-only tools) struggle when models improve and generalize
actionable: Build for broad, general cases and let users adapt to their needs; avoid deeply specialized optimizations that will break when the underlying models improve
source_title: Anthropic's Felix Rieseberg on AI Coworkers, Local-First Agents, and the Future of Knowledge Work
